name: Naram-Sin
description: Naram-Sin, a king of Akkad who reigned c. 2254–2218 BC, is a figure shrouded in both historical significance and mythic interpretation. He was the fourth ruler of the Akkadian Empire, succeeding his father Manishtushu. Naram-Sin significantly expanded the empire through military conquests, claiming dominion over vast territories. He broke with tradition by deifying himself during his reign, a move that was considered audacious and potentially blasphemous by some. This self-deification is a key aspect of his mythic persona, often depicted in later narratives as hubris that ultimately led to the downfall of Akkad. He adopted the title "King of the Four Quarters," signifying his claim to universal rule. His reign marked a shift in Akkadian ideology, emphasizing the king's divine status and absolute authority. Later Mesopotamian sources, particularly those from the Ur III period and beyond, present a complex and often negative portrayal of Naram-Sin, attributing the empire's collapse to his sacrilegious actions, specifically the destruction of the Ekur, the temple of Enlil in Nippur.
subtitle: King of the Four Quarters

keyMyths.title: The Great Revolt
keyMyths.description: This myth recounts a massive rebellion against Naram-Sin's rule, orchestrated by various city-states and foreign powers. The narrative emphasizes the scale of the opposition and the challenges Naram-Sin faced in maintaining control over his vast empire. The revolt is often interpreted as divine punishment for Naram-Sin's self-deification, highlighting the consequences of challenging the traditional divine order. The myth suggests that his hubris alienated both gods and mortals, leading to widespread unrest and instability throughout Akkad. It showcases Naram-Sin's military prowess but also the limits of his power in the face of divine wrath.

keyMyths.title: Naram-Sin and the Destruction of Ekur
keyMyths.description: This myth details Naram-Sin's alleged destruction of the Ekur, the temple of Enlil in Nippur. Angered by Enlil's perceived lack of support during the Great Revolt, Naram-Sin is said to have desecrated and plundered the temple, an act of extreme sacrilege. This act is presented as the ultimate cause of Akkad's downfall, as it incurred Enlil's wrath and led to the withdrawal of divine favor from the empire. The myth serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of hubris and the importance of respecting the gods.

keyMyths.title: The Curse of Agade
keyMyths.description: The Curse of Agade is a literary composition that narrates the fall of Akkad, attributing it to Naram-Sin's impiety. The story describes how Enlil, the chief god, abandoned Akkad after Naram-Sin destroyed the Ekur temple in Nippur. This withdrawal of divine favor resulted in famine, chaos, and ultimately, the destruction of the Akkadian Empire. The myth emphasizes the catastrophic consequences of challenging the established divine order and the importance of maintaining a proper relationship with the gods. It showcases the power of divine retribution and the fragility of even the most powerful empires.
keyMyths.source: Black, J.A., Cunningham, G., Fluckiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., and Taylor, J., The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (http://etcsl.orinst.ox.ac.uk/), Oxford 1998-.
